text: The laparoscopic procedure is initiated through a peri-umbilical port, with two additional twelve millimeter ports placed. A sub-xiphoid incision is made to accommodate a liver retractor, providing access and exposure for the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the operation by taking down the ligamentum teres, followed by the falciform ligament, to facilitate retraction of the left lobe of the liver.

The surgeon dissects and takes down the left triangular ligament, progressing the dissection towards the inferior vena cava. The liver is mobilized sufficiently to allow placement of a Nathanson liver retractor.

The surgeon exposes the veil of tissue overlying the enlarged caudate lobe, with the replaced left hepatic artery visibly flopping over the top. The aberrant left hepatic artery is left in situ due to the abnormal anatomy.
The surgeon performs dissection around the capsule of the liver and the caudate lobe, progressing towards the base of the lesser omentum.
The surgeon identifies large accessory vessels in the surgical field, which are prominently visible during the procedure.

The surgeon continues dissection behind the insertion of the left hepatic vein into the inferior vena cava, proceeding down along the front of the caudate lobe as it merges with the left lobe of the liver in the groove for the ligamentum venosum.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes areolar tissue surrounding the inferior vena cava, revealing the vessel's surface.
The surgeon extends the dissection of fibrous tissue between the caudate lobe and the left lateral section of the liver, aiming to achieve a clear medial margin.
The portacaval shunt is visible beneath the bulk of the chordate lobe, with a tongue of chordate lobe tissue encircling it.
The inferior vena cava comes into view during the laparoscopic procedure, highlighting the advantages of laparoscopic surgery in accessing this anatomical location.
The surgeon takes small caudate vessels near the inferior vena cava, encountering a minor bleed that is controlled using bipolar diathermy.

The surgeon performs further dissection, revealing short gastric and hepatic vessels traveling from the chordate lobe directly into the inferior vena cava. These vessels are secured using blocking haemoclips.
The surgeon uses bipolar diathermy through the Thunderbeak to address small vessels, while larger vessels are visible in the surgical field.
The laparoscopic view provides a clear visualization along the length of the inferior vena cava, allowing the surgeon to identify and dissect small vessels, which are then clipped.
The surgeon uses bipolar diathermy to control the chordate side before cutting through, aiming to minimize the use of clips and avoid clip congestion.
The surgeon divides small tributaries from the hilum to the chordate lobe using bipolar diathermy, focusing on vessels feeding the adenoma, which is visibly expanding the chordate lobe.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the lesser omentum on the undersurface of the replaced hepatic artery. Larger vessels originating from the portal venous structures in the hilum are controlled and dissected using surgical instruments.

The surgeon identifies the main feeding vein and a possibly torn small artery at the back, using a right angle instrument to navigate around them. The torn artery does not cause significant bleeding and is controlled with bipolar diathermy.

The surgeon dissects alongside the shunt, identifying small tributaries running to the chordate lobe. These tributaries are coagulated with bipolar diathermy and cut using the harmonic function of the Thunderbeak instrument. Blunt dissection is employed to develop tissue planes, minimizing parenchymal destruction.

The surgeon performs further dissection along the inferior vena cava, identifying and ligating two or three substantial tributaries from the chordate lobe. The inferior vena cava is carefully dissected off the back of the chordate lobe.

The surgeon attempts to navigate around the bridge of chordate tissue that extends over the front of the shunt down to the inferior vena cava. A tape is passed from behind to visualize the tissue from the front, but the attempt is unsuccessful.

The surgeon performs further dissection from the anterior aspect to approach the inferior vena cava, taking down a small branch from the shunt to the chordate lobe to elongate the shunt. Tape is passed anteriorly to check visibility from the posterior aspect, while continuing dissection.

Using the Olympus three-dimensional laparoscope, the surgeon observes a developing window of areolar tissue to the left of the thunder bead. As the tissue is dissected, a tape becomes visible, indicating the position of the shunt below this point.
The surgeon uses tape for traction on the bridge of chordate tissue over the shunt, opting to delay its division to minimize bleeding and ooze during the operation.

The surgeon performs further dissection near the caudate lobe to control the inflow and carefully peel the caudate lobe away from adjacent vital structures of the remnant liver. The dissection involves finding the correct plane while avoiding the parenchyma of the caudate and left lobe of the liver.
The surgeon identifies a small bleeder from the back of the hepatic artery, which appears to have been pulled during the preservation of the main left hepatic artery.

The surgeon dissects into the bridge of liver tissue where the caudate lobe crosses over to the main liver, positioned in front of and to the right of the inferior vena cava. A small venous bleeder is identified and temporarily controlled with a Raytech sponge.
The surgeon observes the chordate lobe, which appears ischemic with a pale color, indicating the absence of blood flow following successful bleeding control.

The surgeon performs dissection in front of the inferior vena cava and behind the left hepatic vein, revealing the middle hepatic vein joining the left hepatic vein as a common trunk before inserting into the inferior vena cava. The edge of this common trunk is visible as it inserts into the cava.

Using a laparoscope, the surgeon safely passes in front of the vena cava, developing the dissection plane. The superior attachments of the caudate lobe are divided, with the dissection continuing despite the presence of a replaced hepatic artery, which is preserved.

The surgeon begins to dissect the bridge of liver tissue over the front of the shunt using the Thunder Pete device, which functions as both a dissector and vessel sealer. A small vein is identified and addressed using bipolar cautery.

The surgeon carefully places tape to avoid damaging the underlying shunt. With the bridge of tissue removed, the caudate lobe is elevated at a different angle to approach its thinning attachments in front of the inferior vena cava.

The surgeon addresses a few small vessels remaining attached between the porta hepatis and the caudate lobe, carefully maneuvering around the replaced hepatic artery. A suction device is used to manage minor oozing in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses the sucker as a retractor and blunt dissector, alongside the Thunderbead energy device, to manipulate and separate tissue in the surgical field.

A thin bridge of tissue is visible running in front of the inferior vena cava. The surgeon continues to mobilize the cava off the back of this bridge, preparing to divide it with a stapling device. The shunt has moved away from the working area, facilitating the division of the tissue bridge.
The surgeon controls a small vessel by placing a tie around it, facilitating the placement of a Hem-o-lok clip to secure the vessel.
The surgeon uses the Thunder Beat device to seal the remaining tissue on the opposite side, where space is limited, ensuring effective hemostasis.
The surgeon works underneath the caudate lobe, carefully detaching the final tissue attachments towards the porta hepatis, without performing a formal dissection of the porta hepatis itself.

The surgeon uses a stapler to transect a small piece of tissue, ensuring that no clips are caught inside the stapler to prevent misfire. The VATEC is carefully pulled out of the way to avoid interference during the stapling process.

The surgeon divides a small capsule at the back of the caudate lobe to thin out the tissue further. The inferior vena cava is visible, bouncing away at the back, while an artery is slung over the area. The parenchyma appears notably thin.
A fibrous structure, potentially a small vessel, is identified and secured with a hemlock clip. The chordate lobe is visibly detached, while the hepatic artery remains patent.

The surgeon encounters a bleeding point on the parenchymal surface, which is temporarily packed. The specimen is placed in a bag and removed through a small Pfannenstiel incision. After closing the incision, the packing is removed to assess and control the bleeding area.

The surgeon elects to suture the bleeding point deep within the surgical site, as it cannot be controlled with bipolar diathermy. The needle is carefully maneuvered to cinch the suture around the bleeding area, reducing the bleeding. Irrigation is performed, and fibrillar is applied to aid clot formation. The fibrillar is placed near the inferior vena cava, and the liver appears abnormal. The operation is condensed through editing, and the patient is later discharged with healed wounds.
